data.visaNotes: Express Entry is the main route for skilled workers — a points-based system (CRS score) favoring age, education, language, and experience. Current draw cutoffs sit around 480–490. Provincial Nominee Programs can significantly boost CRS scores for those targeting specific provinces.

The EU Blue Card remains the primary route for degree-holders with a job offer (salary threshold ~€45k). The Opportunity Card (Chancenkarte), launched June 2024, lets skilled non-EU nationals enter for 12 months to job-search without a prior offer — requiring either a recognised qualification or 6 points under the points system plus proof of €1,091/month in funds.

Points-based skilled migration (Subclass 189/190). Processing times have improved but remain 6–18 months depending on occupation. The Employer-Sponsored 482 visa is the fastest route for tech workers with a job offer. Critical Skills list updated regularly.

The 30% ruling tax benefit makes the Netherlands very attractive for skilled expats — allowing 30% of gross salary tax-free for 5 years. The Highly Skilled Migrant visa is employer-sponsored with a minimum salary threshold (~€5,600/month gross). Note: the 30% ruling has been capped and is under ongoing review.

The D7 Passive Income Visa requires a minimum €920/month passive income in 2026 (indexed to Portugal's minimum wage). The D8 Digital Nomad Visa requires ~€3,480/month in remote income. Processing now runs through AIMA (replaced SEF) with wait times of 6–12 months for appointments. NHR 2.0 tax regime still available for qualifying new residents.

The Critical Skills Employment Permit is the main route for in-demand roles (tech, healthcare, engineering). English-speaking and home to European HQs of major US tech firms. Dublin rents have risen sharply — budget €2,400+ for a 1BR in the city centre. General Work Permit available for other occupations.

The Skilled Worker visa requires employer sponsorship and a minimum salary of £41,700 (as of July 2025) for most roles, or the going rate for your occupation code, whichever is higher. Minimum skill level raised to RQF Level 6 (degree-equivalent) in July 2025. Global Talent visa available without a job offer for exceptional candidates.

Zero income tax remains the biggest draw. The 10-year Golden Visa expanded in 2025–2026 to include content creators, educators, nurses with 15+ years service, and e-sports professionals, alongside investors (AED 2M) and skilled professionals. The Feb 2026 update removed the 50% down payment requirement for property investors. Standard employment visas remain fast to process via employer sponsorship.

The Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV) is the main employer-sponsored route. The Green List offers fast-track residency for in-demand occupations (nurses, teachers, engineers). Beautiful lifestyle but remote location adds travel cost and adjustment time.

The Digital Nomad Visa (launched 2023) is popular for remote workers requiring ~€2,334/month income and applying through a Spanish consulate. The Beckham Law offers a flat 24% income tax rate for up to 6 years. Non-lucrative visa suits retirees and passive income earners. Barcelona and Madrid rents rising but still below Dublin/London.

Work permit requires a job offer. The employer must advertise the role in the EU/EEA for 10 days and offer market-rate salary with full benefits. Sweden offers excellent work-life balance, generous parental leave, and strong public services. High taxes are offset by free healthcare and education.

Very restrictive immigration for non-EU nationals. Employers must prove no suitable EU/EFTA candidate is available before sponsoring from outside the EU. Annual quotas apply. L-permit (short-term, up to 1 year) and B-permit (residence, renewable) are the main work permits. Salaries are the highest in Europe but costs are proportionally high.

Employment Pass (EP) is the main route for professionals — minimum salary raised to SGD 5,600/month in 2025 for most sectors (SGD 6,200 for finance). The COMPASS framework adds a points-based scoring element. Low tax rates and world-class infrastructure are major draws, though rents remain very high.

H-1B visa is lottery-based with an annual cap of 85,000 — odds around 30% for a single entry. L-1 intracompany transfer is the most reliable route for those already working for a US company abroad. O-1 is available for extraordinary ability. Highest tech salaries globally but immigration uncertainty remains high.

Skilled Worker permit requires a concrete job offer and proof of qualifications. Norway is not in the EU but in the EEA — non-EEA nationals face a full immigration process. Excellent welfare state and quality of life, but the language barrier is real and costs are among the highest in Europe.

The Talent Passport (Passeport Talent) covers multiple categories including employees of innovative companies, researchers, and startup founders. Requires a job offer or proof of eligibility. French language skills greatly help integration. Paris is expensive but other cities like Lyon and Bordeaux offer a good lifestyle-to-cost ratio.

Engineer/Specialist Humanities visa is the main route for tech and professional workers — requires a job offer and relevant degree. The Highly Skilled Professional points system offers fast-track permanent residence in as little as 1–3 years. Japanese language proficiency greatly improves quality of life. Remote-work infrastructure is growing but Japan remains more traditional in workplace culture.

The Decreto Flussi annual quota system controls non-EU work permits and can be competitive. The Intra-company transfer and EU Blue Card routes are more straightforward. Italy offers a Flat Tax regime (€100k/year flat tax) for high-income new residents, and a 70% income tax exemption for new workers who transfer their residency to Italy.

The Red-White-Red Card is Austria's main skilled immigration route — a points-based system for skilled workers, key personnel, and graduates from Austrian universities. Requires a job offer meeting salary thresholds. Vienna consistently ranks as the world's most liveable city. German language skills are essential for daily life.

Single Permit combines a work and residence permit for non-EU nationals. Requires a job offer with a salary meeting regional thresholds. Belgium is home to EU institutions which drives multilingual demand. High taxes are a significant downside, though net salaries remain competitive. Expat tax regime available for some.

The Pay Limit Scheme allows high-earning professionals (DKK 500,000+/year) to obtain a work permit without labour market testing. The Positive List scheme covers in-demand occupations. Denmark has one of the best work-life balances in the world. High taxes fund excellent public services including free healthcare and education.

Finland's D permit (employed) is the main route for non-EU workers — requires a job offer and employer registration. The Fast Track service for specialist workers cuts processing to around 2 weeks. Finland actively recruits tech and healthcare talent and offers strong startup ecosystem support. Finnish language is challenging but English is widely spoken.

Malaysia My Second Home (MM2H) programme offers long-term residency for qualifying applicants. Employment Pass for professionals requires employer sponsorship. Kuala Lumpur offers a very low cost of living for expats with strong English proficiency and a multicultural environment. Tech hub growing rapidly.

Employment Visa required for foreign nationals working in India — employer must be registered and the role typically requires specialised skills not locally available. India is primarily a source of outbound expats rather than inbound. Moving to India suits those with cultural ties, entrepreneurial ambitions, or regional business roles. Fast-growing startup ecosystem in Bangalore, Hyderabad, and Mumbai.

Brazil offers a Digital Nomad Visa for remote workers earning at least USD 1,500/month, valid for 1 year and renewable. Work permits for employed foreigners require employer sponsorship and approval from the Ministry of Labour. Brazil has a large and vibrant expat community in São Paulo and Rio, but safety concerns vary significantly by neighbourhood.

Mexico offers a Temporary Resident Visa (Residente Temporal) for remote workers and professionals, valid for 1–4 years and renewable. Requires proof of income (approx. $2,600 USD/month). If you stay over 183 days per year, you become a tax resident and worldwide income is taxed at progressive rates of 1.92–35%. Foreign income earned before establishing residency is not taxed. Mexico City, Guadalajara, and Oaxaca are top expat hubs with large English-speaking communities.

Thailand's Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) is a 5-year multiple-entry visa for remote workers and digital nomads — stay up to 180 days per entry, renewable. Requires 500,000 THB (~$14,000 USD) in savings or proof of remote employment. The Long-Term Resident (LTR) Visa targets high earners ($80,000+ USD/year) with a 10-year renewable visa and significant tax benefits including exemption on foreign-sourced income. Staying over 180 days triggers Thai tax residency on remitted income.

Greece's Digital Nomad Visa (Type D) allows non-EU remote workers to live in Greece for 1 year, renewable. Requires a minimum monthly income of €3,500 and private health insurance. Key tax benefit: new tax residents employed in Greece receive a 50% income tax exemption for up to 7 years. Digital nomads staying under 183 days owe no Greek tax. Greece is part of Schengen and the EU, giving access to Europe's free movement zone.

Colombia's Digital Nomad Visa (Type V) allows remote workers earning from non-Colombian clients to stay up to 2 years. Minimum income requirement is approximately $1,400 USD/month (3× the minimum wage). If you spend fewer than 183 days per year in Colombia, you are not considered a tax resident and owe no Colombian income tax on foreign earnings. Medellín is the most popular hub — fast-growing tech scene, spring climate year-round, and affordable cost of living.

Panama uses a strict territorial tax system — only income earned inside Panama is taxed. Foreign-sourced income is 100% tax-free regardless of residency. The economy is fully dollarised (USD). The Friendly Nations Visa offers expedited permanent residency to citizens of 50 countries including the US, UK, Canada, and EU states. The Digital Nomad Visa allows an 18-month stay for remote workers earning at least $36,000/year from foreign sources. Ranked #1 globally for expat satisfaction by InterNations 2025.

South Korea's Workation (Digital Nomad) Visa F-1-D launched January 2024 — allows remote workers employed by foreign companies to stay up to 2 years (1 year + 1 renewal). Requires annual income of at least $65,000 USD (2× Korea's GNI per capita) and comprehensive health insurance. Foreign tech workers with a local job offer can apply for the E-7 skilled worker visa. Korea has some of the fastest internet speeds in the world and world-class healthcare.

Czech Republic uses a flat 15% income tax rate for earnings up to CZK 1,762,812/year (~€70k); a 23% rate applies above that. Employees also pay 11% in social and health insurance contributions. Non-EU nationals need a Long-Term Work Permit or EU Blue Card (minimum salary ~€1,600/month). Prague is one of Central Europe's most liveable capitals — affordable compared to Western Europe, full Schengen access, and a rapidly growing tech and startup scene.

Georgia is one of the world's most accessible countries for expats — citizens of 95+ countries can stay visa-free for up to 365 days with no application required. Income earned from foreign clients while living in Georgia is classified as foreign-sourced and is not subject to Georgian personal income tax (20% flat rate applies only to Georgian-sourced income). Freelancers can register as a Small Business (1% tax) on turnover under GEL 500,000/year. A formal Digital Nomad Visa also exists requiring $2,000/month income, free of charge.

Vietnam grants e-visas to all nationalities — valid for 90 days, multiple entry, fully online application at ~$50 USD. For longer stays, the 5-year Talent Visa (special exemption card) targets skilled professionals and allows up to 90 days per stay before renewal. Vietnam is actively courting remote workers and digital tech talent with a new 2025 framework. Ho Chi Minh City and Da Nang are the primary expat hubs offering very low cost of living with fast-improving infrastructure.

Croatia's Digital Nomad Visa offers non-EU remote workers an 18-month stay (renewable once for a total of 3 years). Income requirement as of 2026 is approximately €3,622/month. Crucially, digital nomads working for non-Croatian companies are fully exempt from Croatian income tax on that foreign income. Croatia joined the Schengen Area in January 2023 and adopted the euro, making it a full EU member with all associated travel benefits. Zagreb is affordable relative to Western Europe with a growing tech community.

Costa Rica's Digital Nomad Visa provides a 1-year legal stay, renewable for a second year. Minimum income: $3,000 USD/month for individuals ($4,000 for families). Foreign-sourced income is completely exempt from Costa Rican income tax. Costa Rica runs on a territorial tax system — only locally-earned income is taxed. The CAJA universal healthcare system is available to legal residents. Ranked among the safest and most stable democracies in Latin America with a strong rule of law.

Poland is an EU member with full Schengen access. Non-EU skilled workers can apply for an EU Blue Card (minimum salary PLN 13,355/month gross as of 2026) or a standard work permit sponsored by an employer. Poland has no specific digital nomad visa but offers a Temporary Residence Permit for those working for a Polish entity. Warsaw commands a 15–20% salary premium over other Polish cities and has a rapidly expanding tech ecosystem with major offices from Google, Microsoft, and Samsung. Low cost of living relative to Western Europe.
